teaching.title: Structure of a Basic Program
teaching.analogy: A program is like a recipe card — it reads FROM TOP TO BOTTOM, one line at a time. Line 1 runs first, then Line 2, etc.
teaching.content: Every Python program starts from the top line and runs downward. There's no special setup needed — just write your instructions and Python runs them in order!

examples.title: Top-to-Bottom Execution
examples.code:
print('Line ONE')
print('Line TWO')
print('Line THREE')

teaching.funFact: Python doesn't use semicolons or curly braces — just clean, readable lines! That's why it's popular. 🐍

teaching.title: Print Statements
teaching.analogy: The print() function is like a loudspeaker. You give it text, and it SHOUTS it onto the screen!
teaching.content: print() is the most used function in Python. It takes whatever you put inside the parentheses and displays it on the screen. Text must be inside quotes.

examples.title: Basic Print
examples.explanation: Anything inside quotes is displayed exactly as written.
examples.code:
print('Hello World!')
print("My name is Neo")

teaching.funFact: The very first 'Hello World' program was written in 1978 by Brian Kernighan! 🌍

teaching.title: Output Formatting
teaching.analogy: Output formatting is like decorating a cake — the content stays the same, but you control HOW it looks.
teaching.content: You can print multiple things using commas (adds spaces automatically) or use '+' to glue strings together (no space added). You can also use f-strings for mixing text and variables.

examples.title: Comma vs Plus
examples.explanation: Comma adds a space. Plus glues directly.

examples.title: F-Strings
examples.explanation: f-strings let you embed variables inside text.

teaching.funFact: F-strings were added in Python 3.6 (2016). Before that, formatting was MUCH harder! 😅

mcq.q: What does print('Hello') do?
mcq.explanation: Core Logic: print() is a built-in function that takes data and sends it to the Standard Output (the screen) for you to see. It does not create variables unless assigned to one!

mcq.q: Which of these will Python run FIRST?
mcq.explanation: Sequential Sync: Python is an interpreted language that reads your instructions exactly like a book—starting at line 1 and moving down systematically.

mcq.q: What is the output of: print('5' + '3')?
mcq.explanation: String Logic: Because 5 and 3 are in quotes, Python treats them as characters (text), not math. The + operator glues them together ('concatenation') to make 53.

mcq.q: What does the 'f' in f'Hello {name}' do?
options: Formats the string so variables inside {} are replaced with their values
mcq.explanation: F-String Magic: The 'f' stands for Format. It tells Python to 'look inside' the string for curly braces {} and swap them with the actual values stored in your variables.

mcq.q: Text inside print() must be wrapped in ___.
mcq.explanation: Syntax Rule: Text (strings) must always be inside quotes ('' or ""). Without them, Python thinks you are trying to use a variable name that doesn't exist yet!

coding.title: Your First Print
coding.objective: What function displays text on the screen in Python? Type just the function name.
coding.hint: It rhymes with 'mint' and starts with 'p'.

coding.title: Fix the Quote
coding.objective: What kind of characters must surround text in a print statement? Type the answer.
coding.hint: Either single ' or double " characters wrap text.

coding.title: Predict the Glue
coding.objective: What does print('Code' + 'Clash') output? Type the exact output.
coding.hint: The + operator glues strings together WITHOUT adding a space.

debugging.description:
🚨 This code has an error:

Print('Hello World')

What is the bug?
options: The 'P' in Print should be lowercase: print
debugging.answer: The 'P' in Print should be lowercase: print
debugging.hint: Python is CASE SENSITIVE. Built-in functions are all lowercase.

debugging.description:
🚨 This code has an error:

print(Hello World)

What is the bug?
debugging.hint: Text must be in quotes: 'Hello World' or "Hello World".

prediction.explanation: Python runs top-to-bottom. Line 1 prints 'First', Line 2 prints 'Second', Line 3 prints 'Third'.

teaching.title: What Are Variables?
teaching.analogy: A variable is a labeled box. You give it a name (the label) and store a value (the contents) inside it. Later, you can peek inside using the label.
teaching.content: Variables store data in your computer's memory with a name. You can create them, change them, and use them anytime. In Python, you just write: name = value.

examples.title: Creating Variables
examples.explanation: The = sign means 'put this value into this box'.

teaching.funFact: Unlike Java or C, Python figures out the data type automatically — you never need to declare it! 🧠

teaching.title: Naming Rules for Variables
teaching.analogy: Variable names are like hashtags: they can have letters, numbers, and underscores, but NO spaces and can't start with a number.
teaching.content: Rules: 1) Must start with a letter or underscore. 2) Can contain letters, numbers, underscores. 3) NO spaces or special characters. 4) Case sensitive (Score ≠ score). 5) Can't use Python keywords (if, for, while).

examples.title: Valid vs Invalid Names
examples.code:
✅ my_score = 10
✅ _name = 'Alex'
✅ player2 = 'Neo'
❌ 2player = 'Bad'   # starts with number
❌ my score = 'Bad'  # has a space
❌ if = 10           # 'if' is a keyword

teaching.funFact: Python has 35 reserved keywords you can't use as variable names. 'if', 'else', 'for' are some! 🚫

teaching.title: Assigning Values
teaching.analogy: The = sign in Python doesn't mean 'equals' like in math. It means 'PUT this value INTO this box'. Think of it as an arrow: box ← value.
teaching.content: Assignment uses = to store values. You can assign numbers, text, booleans, or even calculations. Python evaluates the RIGHT side first, then stores it LEFT.

examples.title: Assignment Flow
examples.explanation: Right side is calculated first, then stored in the left.
examples.code:
x = 5 + 3        # x stores 8
name = 'Neo'      # name stores 'Neo'
is_hero = True    # is_hero stores True

teaching.funFact: You can assign multiple variables in one line: a, b, c = 1, 2, 3 🎯

mcq.q: What does this line do? score = 100
options: Creates a variable named 'score' and stores 100 in it
mcq.explanation: The = sign means 'Store the value 100 into a variable called score'.

mcq.q: Which of these is a VALID variable name?
mcq.explanation: player_name uses only letters and underscores, starts with a letter — valid! Others break rules.

mcq.q: After x = 5 + 3, what is x?
mcq.explanation: Python calculates 5 + 3 = 8 on the right, then stores 8 in x.

mcq.q: Is 'Score' the same as 'score' in Python?
options: No, Python is case sensitive — they are different variables
mcq.explanation: Python treats uppercase and lowercase as different. Score ≠ score ≠ SCORE.

mcq.q: Why can't you use 'if' as a variable name?
mcq.explanation: 'if' is a Python keyword used for conditions. Keywords can't be used as variable names.

coding.title: Create a Variable
coding.objective: Create a variable called 'age' that stores the number 12. What goes on the right side of: age = ___?
coding.hint: Just type the number. No quotes needed for numbers.

coding.title: Spot the Valid Name
coding.objective: Is '_secret' a valid variable name? Type 'yes' or 'no'.
coding.hint: Variable names CAN start with an underscore.

coding.title: Calculate & Assign
coding.objective: What value is stored in 'result' after this code? result = 10 * 3
coding.hint: Python calculates 10 * 3 = 30, then stores 30 in result.

options: Variable names can't START with a number — rename to score1
debugging.answer: Variable names can't START with a number — rename to score1
debugging.hint: Rule: Variable names must start with a letter or underscore, never a number.

debugging.description:
🚨 This code has an error:

my name = 'Alex'
options: Names can't have spaces — use my_name instead
Alex should not be in quotes
debugging.answer: Names can't have spaces — use my_name instead
debugging.hint: Variable names cannot contain spaces. Use underscores instead.

prediction.explanation: x stores 10, y stores 20. x + y = 10 + 20 = 30 (number addition).
name stores 'Code', tag stores 'Clash'. + glues strings: 'CodeClash' (no space).

teaching.title: The 4 Basic Data Types
teaching.analogy: Data types are like jars in a kitchen: one jar holds flour (integers), one holds syrup (floats), one holds labels (strings), and one is a switch (boolean — ON or OFF).
teaching.content: Python has 4 fundamental types: int (whole numbers), float (decimal numbers), str (text), and bool (True or False). Python auto-detects the type — you don't declare it!

examples.title: The 4 Types
examples.explanation: Each type stores a different kind of data.
examples.code:
age = 18          # int (whole number)
price = 9.99      # float (decimal)
name = 'Alex'     # str (text in quotes)
is_alive = True   # bool (True or False)

teaching.funFact: You can check any variable's type using type(). Try type(3.14) — it returns <class 'float'>! 🔍

teaching.title: Integers & Floats
teaching.analogy: Integers are coins (whole, no fractions). Floats are liquid — they can fill any decimal point.
teaching.content: int: 0, 5, -100, 9999. No decimal point. float: 3.14, 0.5, -2.7. Always has a decimal. Mixing int + float gives a float result.

examples.title: Int vs Float Math
examples.explanation: When you mix them, Python converts to float.

teaching.funFact: Division (/) ALWAYS returns a float in Python 3, even 10 / 2 = 5.0! Use // for integer division. 🤓

teaching.title: Strings & Booleans
teaching.analogy: Strings are necklaces of characters strung together. Booleans are light switches — either ON (True) or OFF (False), nothing in between.
teaching.content: Strings hold text wrapped in quotes. Booleans hold exactly one of two values: True or False (capital T and F). Booleans are the foundation of all decisions in programming.

examples.title: Strings & Bools
examples.explanation: Note: True and False must be capitalized!
examples.code:
greeting = 'Hello'
is_cool = True
is_boring = False

# WRONG:
# is_cool = true    # ERROR! Capital T needed

teaching.funFact: In Python, empty strings '' are 'falsy' — they act like False in conditions! 🤯

mcq.q: What data type is 3.14?
mcq.explanation: 3.14 has a decimal point — that makes it a float (floating-point number).

mcq.q: What data type is 'Hello'?
mcq.explanation: Text inside quotes is always a string (str).

mcq.q: What does print(type(True)) show?
mcq.explanation: True is a boolean value, so type() returns <class 'bool'>.

mcq.q: What is the result of 10 / 2 in Python 3?
mcq.explanation: In Python 3, the / operator always returns a float: 10 / 2 = 5.0.

mcq.q: Which is NOT a valid boolean in Python?
options: Both True and False are valid
mcq.explanation: 'true' (lowercase) is NOT a boolean — Python requires capital T: True.

coding.title: Name the Type
coding.objective: What data type is the number 42? Type: int, float, str, or bool.
coding.hint: 42 is a whole number with no decimal point.

coding.title: True or False?
coding.objective: What is the correct Python boolean for 'yes/on'? Type it exactly.

coding.title: Float Result
coding.objective: What does print(7 + 3.0) output? Type the exact output.
coding.hint: Mixing int + float always gives a float result.

debugging.description:
🚨 This code has a bug:

is_admin = true
debugging.hint: Python booleans require capital letters: True and False.

options: Can't add a string '18' to a number 2 — Remove quotes: age = 18
debugging.answer: Can't add a string '18' to a number 2 — Remove quotes: age = 18
debugging.hint: You can't mix string + int. Either both must be strings or both must be numbers.

prediction.explanation: '5' and '3' are strings (in quotes), so + concatenates them: '53'.
/ always returns a float in Python 3. 10 / 3 = 3.3333333333333335.

teaching.title: Reassigning Variables
teaching.analogy: Reassigning a variable is like erasing a whiteboard and writing something new. The old value is GONE forever.
teaching.content: You can change a variable's value at any time by assigning a new value with =. The old value is permanently overwritten. You can even change the data type!

examples.title: Overwrite in Action
examples.explanation: x starts as 10, then becomes 20. The 10 is gone.
examples.code:
x = 10
print(x)   # 10
x = 20
print(x)   # 20 (10 is gone!)

examples.title: Self-Update
examples.explanation: You can use the old value to calculate the new value!

teaching.funFact: In Python, you can even change a variable's type: x = 5 then x = 'hello' — totally legal! 🤯

teaching.title: Basic Input (Concept)
teaching.analogy: input() is like a pause button — the program STOPS and waits for the user to type something, then stores what they typed.
teaching.content: The input() function pauses the program, shows a message, waits for the user to type, and stores whatever they type as a STRING. Always a string — even if they type numbers!

examples.title: Getting User Input
examples.code:
name = input('What is your name? ')
print('Hello', name)

# User types: Alex
# Output: Hello Alex

teaching.funFact: input() ALWAYS returns a string. To get a number, you must convert: int(input('Age: ')). ⌨️

mcq.q:
After this code, what is x?
x = 5
x = 10
mcq.explanation: The second line overwrites x. The old value 5 is gone. x is now 10.

mcq.q: What does hp = hp - 20 do?
options: Checks if hp equals hp minus 20
Subtracts 20 from hp and stores the result back in hp
mcq.explanation: Python calculates hp - 20 (right side), then stores the result back into hp (left side).

mcq.q: What does input() return?
mcq.explanation: input() ALWAYS returns a string, even if the user types a number like '42'.

mcq.q: What happens when input() runs?
options: The program pauses and waits for the user to type something
mcq.explanation: input() is a pause — the program waits until the user types and presses Enter.

coding.title: Overwrite the Score
coding.objective: score = 100, then score = score + 50. What is score now?
coding.hint: 100 + 50 = 150. The new value replaces the old one.

coding.title: Input Returns What?
coding.objective: User types '25' into input(). What type is the result? Type: int, float, str, or bool.
coding.hint: input() ALWAYS returns a string — even if the user types numbers.

options: x + 5 calculates 15 but doesn't save it — need x = x + 5
debugging.answer: x + 5 calculates 15 but doesn't save it — need x = x + 5
debugging.hint: x + 5 alone does nothing. You must STORE the result: x = x + 5.

options: input() returns a string, so age + 1 fails. Need: int(age) + 1
debugging.answer: input() returns a string, so age + 1 fails. Need: int(age) + 1
debugging.hint: You can't add a string '25' + number 1. Convert first: int(age).

prediction.code:
hp = 100
hp = hp - 30
hp = hp - 20
print(hp)

prediction.explanation: b = a copies the VALUE of a (5) into b. Changing a later doesn't affect b.

teaching.title: Comments in Code
teaching.analogy: Comments are sticky notes for humans. Python completely ignores them — they're invisible to the computer but GOLD for programmers reading your code later.
teaching.content: Add a # before any line to make it a comment. Python skips it entirely. Use comments to explain tricky code, leave notes, or temporarily disable code.

examples.title: Comment Examples
examples.explanation: Everything after # is invisible to Python.
examples.code:
# This calculates the damage
damage = 50 + 25
print(damage)  # This prints 75

teaching.funFact: Good programmers write comments explaining WHY, not WHAT. The code already shows what — comments explain the reasoning! 💡

teaching.title: Keywords & Case Sensitivity
teaching.analogy: Keywords are like reserved parking spots — only Python can use them. Case sensitivity means 'Alex' ≠ 'alex' ≠ 'ALEX' — every letter's case matters.
teaching.content: Python has 35 keywords (if, else, for, while, True, False, etc.) you CANNOT use as variable names. Also, Python is case sensitive: print ≠ Print ≠ PRINT.

examples.title: Keywords & Case
examples.explanation: The case of every letter matters in Python.
examples.code:
# These are ALL different:
score = 10
Score = 20
SCORE = 30
print(score)  # 10
print(Score)  # 20
print(SCORE)  # 30

teaching.funFact: Python 3 has exactly 35 reserved keywords. 'print' used to be a keyword in Python 2 but became a function in Python 3! 🐍

mcq.q: What does the # symbol do in Python?
options: Starts a comment — Python ignores everything after it
mcq.explanation: # starts a comment. Python completely ignores everything after # on that line.

mcq.q: Which of these is a Python keyword?
mcq.explanation: 'if' is a reserved keyword used for conditions. You can't use it as a variable name.

mcq.q: In Python, are 'Name' and 'name' the same variable?
options: No, they are completely different variables
Same in Python 3, different in Python 2
mcq.explanation: Python is case sensitive. Different capitalization = different variables.

mcq.q: What happens if you write: If x > 5: ?
options: It creates a variable called If
mcq.explanation: 'If' (capital I) is NOT a keyword. Only lowercase 'if' is the keyword. This causes an error.

coding.title: Write a Comment
coding.objective: What character starts a comment in Python? Type the single character.
coding.hint: It's a single character used before lines Python should ignore.

coding.title: Case Check
coding.objective:
After this code, what does print(name) output?

name = 'Alex'
Name = 'Sam'
coding.hint: 'name' and 'Name' are DIFFERENT variables. print(name) uses the lowercase one.

coding.title: Keyword Check
coding.objective: Can you use 'while' as a variable name? Type 'yes' or 'no'.
coding.hint: 'while' is a reserved Python keyword for loops.

options: 'If' must be lowercase 'if' — Python keywords are all lowercase
debugging.answer: 'If' must be lowercase 'if' — Python keywords are all lowercase
debugging.hint: Python is case sensitive. All keywords are lowercase: if, else, for, while, etc.

options: 'Score' and 'score' are different — score (lowercase) was never created, so Error
debugging.answer: 'Score' and 'score' are different — score (lowercase) was never created, so Error
debugging.hint: You created 'Score' (capital S) but tried to print 'score' (lowercase s) — they're different!

prediction.explanation: The first line is a comment (starts with #) so Python skips it entirely. Only 'Visible' is printed.
X = 10 and x = 20 are different variables (case sensitive). X + x = 10 + 20 = 30.

teaching.title: ⚠️ BOSS — THE COMPILER
teaching.analogy: The Compiler checks every line of your code. One mistake and it REJECTS everything. Prove you've mastered the basics!
teaching.content: THE COMPILER has absorbed all of Level 1's knowledge: print statements, variables, data types, reassignment, comments, and case sensitivity. Defeat it to earn your 'First Code' badge!

examples.title: What you've mastered
examples.explanation: Everything from Level 1 will be tested!
examples.code:
✅ print() & formatting
✅ Variables & naming rules
✅ int, float, str, bool
✅ Reassignment & input
✅ Comments, keywords, case

teaching.funFact: A real compiler translates ALL your code to machine language before running it. Python uses an interpreter instead (runs line by line)! ⚡

mcq.q: What is the output of print(10 + '5')?
options: Error — can't add int and str
mcq.explanation: You can't add an integer (10) and a string ('5'). Python throws a TypeError.

mcq.q:
After this code, what is result?
result = 10
result = result * 2
result = result - 5
mcq.explanation: 10 → 10*2=20 → 20-5=15. Each line overwrites result.

mcq.q:
Which line has a bug?
Line 1: name = 'Alex'
Line 2: Print(name)
mcq.explanation: Python is case sensitive. 'Print' is not a function — only 'print' (lowercase) works.

mcq.q: What does type('True') return?
mcq.explanation: 'True' is in QUOTES, so it's a string — not a boolean! type('True') → <class 'str'>.

mcq.q:
x = 5
# x = 10
print(x)
mcq.explanation: Line 2 is a comment (starts with #), so Python ignores it. x stays as 5.

coding.title: Boss Challenge: What's the Type?
coding.objective: What data type is the value False? Type: int, float, str, or bool.
coding.hint: False (with capital F) is one of the two boolean values.

coding.title: Boss Challenge: Chain Math
coding.objective: x = 5, then x = x * 4, then x = x - 10. What is x?
coding.starterCode:
x = 5
x = x * 4
x = x - 10
print(x)
# Output: ___

options: 'for' is a reserved keyword — you can't use it as a variable name
debugging.answer: 'for' is a reserved keyword — you can't use it as a variable name
debugging.hint: 'for' is used for loops in Python — it's a reserved keyword.

prediction.code:
a = '3'
b = 3
# print(a + b)
print(a * b)
prediction.explanation: Line 3 is a comment. a * b = '3' * 3 = '333' (string repeated 3 times!).
